.\docs\release_notes_v1.0.md:83:The `M-INT-4` LLM scope classifier and `M-INT-5` domain router run in **telemetry-only mode in v1.0** (see `scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1055-1097`). They observe and log routing decisions but do not gate retrieval. Production routing decisions are made by the curated `template_classifier`. M-INT-4/5 enforcement is a v1.1 milestone.
.\outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_4\findings.md:75:There is also a status interaction: even when the outline collapses to one section, the orchestrator can still write `success` or an unrelated `partial_*` status. .\outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_4\findings.md:79:The fallback is not useless. In the AF anticoagulation run, the one-section fallback produced 4 verified sentences, 0 dropped sentences, a bibliography, and a report that Qwen judged mostly `good`/`acceptable`. .\outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_4\findings.md:81:The problem is observability and breadth, not simply prose validity. The same report is only 163 generator words, has one findings section, cites one bibliography entry, and still ships under `ok_thin_corpus` rather than an outline-specific degradation status. The artifact scan is also concerning: among 19 manifests under `outputs` with `generator.outline_sections`, 10 have fewer than 3 sections and 8 have exactly one section. Some older artifacts have blank section names, so this is not clean production rate evidence, but it is enough to reject "abort immediately on first planner miss" as too blunt and to reject silent fallback as unsafe.

To view a copy of this license, visit <http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/>.\n[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\nPMCID: PMC6455940 NIHMSID: [NIHMS1512873](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/mid/NIHMS1512873/) PMID: [30622333](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30622333/)\n**This article has been corrected.** See [J Expo Sci Environ Epidemiol. 2019 Mar 29;29(6):861](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6803515/).\n**This article has been corrected.** See [J Expo Sci Environ Epidemiol. 2020 Feb 6;30(3):585](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8076034/).\n##  Abstract\nCommunities across the U.S. are discovering drinking water contaminated by perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) and determining appropriate actions. There are currently no federal PFAS drinking water standards despite widespread drinking water contamination, ubiquitous population-level exposure, and toxicological and epidemiological evidence of adverse health effects. Absent federal PFAS standards, multiple U.S. states have developed their own health-based water guideline levels to guide decisions about contaminated site cleanup and drinking water surveillance and treatment. We examined perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) water guideline levels developed by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and state agencies to protect people drinking"

All content is public domain unless otherwise noted.\n[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\nPMCID: PMC7657368 PMID: [33174763](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33174763/)\nSee \"[Serum Half-Lives for Short- and Long-Chain Perfluoroalkyl Acids after Ceasing Exposure from Drinking Water Contaminated by Firefighting Foam](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7351026/)\", 077004.\n##  Abstract\n![A firefighter spraying foam at a jet](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/93d2/7657368/e4d845e44e11/ehp7853_abstract.jpg)\n[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7657368/figure/d40e81/)\n* * *\nMany studies have examined the impact of long-chain per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) on human health,[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7657368/#c1) but few have looked at short-chain PFAS.[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7657368/#c2) A recent study in _Environmental Health Perspectives_ estimated half-lives of both long- and short-chain PFAS in a small group of people immediately after exposure to PFAS-contaminated drinking water ended.[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7657368/#c3)\n![A firefighter spraying foam at a jet](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/93d2/7657368/cbaf044ed924/ehp7853_f1.jpg)\n[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7657368/figure/f1/)\nThe same chemistry that makes PFAS so persistent in the environment also enables them to quickly extinguish fires. Firefighting foam spreads and forms a film over the fire, smothering the flames and cooling the fuel. Image: © Dushlik/Shutterstock.\nThe terms “long” and “short” refer to the length of the carbon (C) backbone of each molecule; long-chain is defined as perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids with 6 or more C atoms, and perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids with 7 or more C atoms.[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7657368/#c4) Most long-chain PFAS have been phased out and replaced by short-chain varieties. Thus, long-chain varieties are sometimes called legacy PFAS.\nIt is generally believed that the shorter the chain, the shorter the half-life for PFAS.[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7657368/#c2) However, this rapid excretion also makes it difficult to assess the health impacts of short-chain varieties because studies may start weeks or months after exposure has ceased.\n“For an observational study of half-life in humans, it is crucial to find a proper population with a defined end of external exposure,” says lead author Yiyi Xu, a research associate at the University of Gothenburg in Sweden. “Due to faster elimination of short-chain PFAS, researchers need to start the observation as soon as the external exposure stops, to capture reasonable serum levels for half-life estimation.” Only a handful of previous studies have quantified the half-lives of short-chain PFAS in human populations.[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7657368/#c5),[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7657368/#c6),[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7657368/#c7),[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7657368/#c8)\nThe new study took place at Sweden’s Arvidsjaur regional airport, which has its own drinking water supply, separate from the municipality of Arvidsjaur. The airport water source was contaminated with PFAS, presumably from aqueous film-forming foam used at an on-site fire drill area. Some firefighting foams contain fluorinated surfactants and are a known source of both long- and short-chain PFAS pollution.[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7657368/#c9)\nWhen authorities discovered the water contamination at the airport in August 2018, they immediately provided clean water to employees and then contacted the authors of the new paper to quantify exposure levels. The researchers sampled serum and urine for 17 airport employees starting 11–14 days after exposure ended. The municipal supply had very low levels of PFAS, which enabled the researchers to confirm that employees"

Germany\nFind articles by [Peter Grathwohl](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Grathwohl%20P\"\\[Author\\])\n†\n  * Author information\n  * Article notes\n  * Copyright and License information\n\n†Department of Geosciences, University of Tübingen, Schnarrenbergstraße 94-96, Tübingen 72076, Germany\n‡Department of Chemical Engineering and Analytical Chemistry, University of joel.fabregat-palau@uni-tuebingen.de.\n*\nE-mail: amirhossein.ershadi@uni-tuebingen.de.\nReceived 2024 Nov 28; Accepted 2025 Mar 31; Revised 2025 Mar 29; Collection date 2025 Apr 22.\n© 2025 The Authors. Published by American Chemical Society\nPermits the broadest form of re-use including for commercial purposes, provided that author attribution and integrity are maintained (<https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/>).\n[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\nPMCID: PMC12020356 PMID: [40215413](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40215413/)\n##  Abstract\n![graphic file with name es4c13284_0006.jpg](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/5c58/12020356/9fc51de0fb93/es4c13284_0006.jpg)\nIn this study, we introduce PFASorptionML, a novel machine learning (ML) tool developed to predict solid–liquid distribution coefficients (_K_ d) for per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in soils. Leveraging a data set of 1,274 _K_ d entries for PFAS in soils and sediments, including compounds such as trifluoroacetate, cationic, and zwitterionic PFAS, and neutral fluorotelomer alcohols, the model incorporates PFAS-specific properties such as molecular weight, hydrophobicity, and p _K_ a, alongside soil characteristics like pH, texture, organic carbon content, and cation exchange capacity. Sensitivity analysis reveals that molecular weight, hydrophobicity, and organic carbon content are the most significant factors influencing sorption behavior, while charge density and mineral soil fraction have comparatively minor effects. The model demonstrates high predictive performance, with RPD values exceeding 3.16 across validation data sets, outperforming existing tools in accuracy and scope. Notably, PFAS chain length and functional group variability significantly influence _K_ d, with longer chain lengths and higher hydrophobicity positively correlating with _K_ d. By integrating location-specific soil repository data, the model enables the generation of spatial _K_ d maps for selected PFAS species. These capabilities are implemented in the online platform PFASorptionML, providin"

Phillips, 4458 TAMU, College Station, TX. 77843-4458; Phone: 979-229-1585, tphillips@cvm.tamu.edu\nIssue date 2022 Apr 1.\n[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\nPMCID: PMC8760172 NIHMSID: NIHMS1763191 PMID: [34875259](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34875259/)\nThe publisher's version of this article is available at [Environ Res](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envres.2021.112433)\n##  Abstract\nConsumption of food and water contaminated with per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) presents a significant risk for human exposure. There is limited data on high affinity sorbents that can be used to reduce the bioavailability of PFAS from soil and translocation to plants and garden produce. To address this need, montmorillonite clay was amended with the nutrients carnitine and choline to increase the hydrophobicity of the sorbent and the interlayer spacing. In this study, the binding of PFOA (perfluorooctanoic acid) and PFOS (perfluorooctanesulfonic acid) to parent and amended clays was characterized. Isothermal analyses were conducted at pH 7 and ambient temperature (to simulate environmentally-relevant conditions). The data for all tested sorbents fit the Langmuir model indicating saturable binding sites with high capacities and affinities under neutral conditions. Amended montmorillonite clays had increased capacities for PFOA and PFOS (0.51 – 0.71 mol kg−1) compared to the parent clay (0.37 – 0.49 mol kg−1). Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations suggested that hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions at the terminal fluorinated carbon chains of PFAS compounds were major modes of surface interaction. The safety and efficacy of the clays were confirmed in a living organism (_Lemna minor_), where clays (at 0.1% inclusion) allowed for increase"

Comparing PFAS analysis in batch leaching and column leaching tests [Ute Kalbe](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Kalbe%20U\"\\[Author\\]) ### Ute Kalbe 1BAM Federal Institute for Materials Research and Testing: Bundesanstalt für Materialforschung und -prüfung, 12200 Berlin, Germany Find articles by [Ute Kalbe](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Kalbe%20U\"\\[Author\\]) 1,✉, [Christian Piechotta](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Piechotta%20C\"\\[Author\\]) ### Christian Piechotta 1BAM Federal Institute for Materials Research and Testing: Bundesanstalt für Materialforschung und -prüfung, 12200 Berlin, Germany Find articles by [Christian Piechotta](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Piechotta%20C\"\\[Author\\]) 1, [Nicole Bandow](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Bandow%20N\"\\[Author\\]) ### Nicole Bandow 1BAM Federal Institute for Materials Research and Testing: Bundesanstalt für Materialforschung und -prüfung, 12200 Berlin, Germany 2Present Address: German Environment Agency, Colditzstraße 34, 12099 Berlin, Germany Find articles by [Nicole Bandow](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Bandow%20N\"\\[Author\\]) 1,2 * Author information * Article notes * Copyright and License information 1BAM Federal Institute for Materials Research and Testing: Bundesanstalt für Materialforschung und -prüfung, 12200 Berlin, Germany 2Present Address: German Environment Agency, Colditzstraße 34, 12099 Berlin, Germany Responsible Editor: Roland Peter Kallenborn ✉ Corresponding author. Received 2023 Dec 15; Accepted 2024 Oct 31; Issue date 2024. © The Author(s) 2024 **Open Access** This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License, which permits use, sharing, adaptation, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if changes were made. [PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/) PMCID: PMC11631822 PMID: [39576439](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39576439/) ## Abstract Laboratory leaching tests are tools to assess the mobility of environmental contaminants released from granular materials. Comparative leaching tests were performed using four PFAS-contaminated soils whose concentration patterns of 10 selected perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) differed due to the two types of contamination sources. This study aimed to evaluate the equivalence of two usual laboratory-scale leaching test procedures, batch and column percolation tests, at liquid-to-solid ratios (L/S) of 2 l/kg, which is the current practice within the German assessment framework, and 10 l/kg (relevant for some EU regulations such as the landfill directive). The differences between the replicates of leaching tests investigating PFAS were smaller for column percolation tests than for batch tests, probably mainly due to the greater sample size and the better representativeness of the sample portion analyzed. It was observed that batch tests overestimate the release of shorter-chain PFAS, whereby the effect was greater with carboxylic than with sulfonic acids. Currently, the limits of detection of analyses given by the DIN standard with regard to PFCA and PFSA in soils are partly not sufficient to detect very low contents, whereas the detection of selected PFCA and PFSA in eluates is more sensitive, in accordance with the available standards. This results in limitations when calculating mass balances. ### Supplementary Information The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1007/s11356-024-35510-0. **Keywords:** PFAS, Leaching, Column percolation test, Batch test, Eluate pretreatment, Soil ## Background Perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are widely used due to their ability to provide surfaces with beneficial properties, such as repellency to water, grease, and dirt. Fields of applications include their use as agents in firefighting foams, paper products, galvanic processes, outd"

[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/) PMCID: PMC11950043 PMID: [40146386](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40146386/) ## Abstract Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are persistent environmental contaminants widely detected in water and sediment worldwide. Despite growing concerns about their ecological and health risks, their distribution in African aquatic environments remains understudied. This study addresses the knowledge gap in PFAS contamination by analysing the spatial and temporal distribution of 18 PFAS in Apies River water and sediment in Pretoria, South Africa. Surface water and sediment samples were collected upstream and downstream of the Apies River during dry seasons. The analysis of PFAS concentrations was conducted using liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry. Statistical analysis, including paired t-tests, non-metric multidimensional scaling, and hierarchical cluster analysis, were applied t"

search](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12917841/?term=\"ACS%20Omega\"\\[jour\\])\n\n# Perfluoroalkyl and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Release from Biosolid-Derived Compost\n[Xiangui Huang](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Huang%20X\"\\[Author\\])\n### Xiangui Huang\n† Zuckerberg Institute for Water Research, The Jacob Blaustein Institutes for Desert Research, Ben-Gurion University of the Negev, Sede-Boqer Campus, Sde-Boqer 8499000, Israel \nFind articles by [Xiangui Huang](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Huang%20X\"\\[Author\\])\n†, [Gilboa Arye](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Arye%20G\"\\[Author\\])\n### Gilboa Arye\n‡ French Associates Institute for Agriculture and Biotechnology of Drylands, The Jacob Blaustein Institutes for Desert Research, Ben-Gurion University of the Negev, Sede-Boqer Campus, Be’er Sheva 8499000, Israel \nFind articles by [Gilboa Arye](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Arye%20G\"\\[Author\\])\n‡, [Wen Zhang](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Zhang%20W\"\\[Author\\])\n### Wen Zhang\n§ Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, New Jersey Institute of Technology, Newark, New Jersey 07102, United States \nFind articles by [Wen Zhang](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Zhang%20W\"\\[Author\\])\n§, [Avner Ronen](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Ronen%20A\"\\[Author\\])\n### Avner Ronen\n† Zuckerberg Institute for Water Research, The Jacob Blaustein Institutes for Desert Research, Ben-Gurion University of the Negev, Sede-Boqer Campus, Sde-Boqer 8499000, Israel \nFind articles by [Avner Ronen](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Ronen%20A\"\\[Author\\])\n†,*\n  * Author information\n  * Article notes\n  * Copyright and License information\n\n† Zuckerberg Institute for Water Research, The Jacob Blaustein Institutes for Desert Research, Ben-Gurion University of the Negev, Sede-Boqer Campus, Sde-Boqer 8499000, Israel \n‡ French Associates Institute for Agriculture and Biotechnology of Drylands, The Jacob Blaustein Institutes for Desert Research, Ben-Gurion University of the Negev, Sede-Boqer Campus, Be’er Sheva 8499000, Israel \n§ Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, New Jersey Institute of Technology, Newark, New Jersey 07102, United States \n*\nEmail: avnerr@bgu.ac.il.\nReceived 2025 Oct 21; Accepted 2026 Jan 22; Revised 2026 Jan 11; Collection date 2026 Feb 17.\n© 2026 The Authors. Published by American Chemical Society\nThis article is licensed under CC-BY 4.0\n[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\nPMCID: PMC12917841 PMID: [41726744](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41726744/)\n##  Abstract\nThis study investigated the release potential and controlling mechanisms of representative per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) of varying carbon-chain lengths from a commercial biosolid-derived compost using sequential leaching with water and saline solutions (10 mM NaCl and 5 mM CaCl2). The compost contained >40% organic matter, PFAS concentrations up to 140 ng·g–1, and precursor levels below 5 ng·g–1. Eluate analyses revealed dissolved organic matter (DOM) concentrations up to 1400 mg·L–1 and major ions (Ca, Mg, Na, K, Cl, P, and S) reaching 600 mg·L–1, accompanied by PFAS concentrations up to 2600 ng·L–1. PFAS desorption followed a biphasic patternrapid release within the first hour followed by a slower, sustained phase over 48 hwell described by a first-order two-compartment model with rate constants _k_ 1 = 1–7 h–1 and _k_ 2 = 0.001–0.016 h–1. Electrostatic interactions dominated the desorption process, as both Na+ and Ca2+ reduced the fraction of fast-release sites (_F_ 1), with Ca2+ showing a stronger suppression due to cation bridging and charge screening. Perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA) and perfluorohexanesulfonic acid (PFHxS) were almost completely released within 1 h, whereas perfluorobutanoic acid (PFBA), perfluorobutanesulfonic acid (PFBS), perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), and perfluorooctanesulfonate (PFOS) exhibited prolonged desorption associated with interactions with solid organic matter and DOM. DOM-facilitated desorption was evident, as PFAS–DOM complexation enhanced release under certain ionic conditions. Overall, the results reveal the intricate coupling among compost matrix properties, PFAS molecular structure, and eluent chemistry governing PFAS mobility.\n* * *\n[![graphic file with name ao5c11002_0007.jpg](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/ebd3/12917841/51dfaa700df3/ao5c11002_0007.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=12917841_ao5c11002_0007.jpg)\n* * *\n[![graphic file with name ao5c11002_0005.jpg](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/ebd3/12917841/51dfaa700df3/ao5c11002_0005.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm."

.\outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_1\stderr.log:1476:The spec I’m about to write treats `summary["status"]` as a secondary sweep/reporting field and makes `manifest.status` the authoritative contract. .\outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_1\stderr.log:1494:+  `run_one_query` currently has four `manifest.json` write sites, but only the three abort manifests include `status`; the success manifest is written before the later summary-only status calculation. .\outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_1\stderr.log:1688:  `run_one_query` currently has four `manifest.json` write sites, but only the three abort manifests include `status`; the success manifest is written before the later summary-only status calculation. Two additional exits, zero retrieved sources and the broad exception handler, return without any manifest at all. The fix should make `manifest.status` the single authoritative run verdict while preserving `summary["status"]` as backward-compatible sweep telemetry.

715:def _parse_adequacy(raw: Any) -> AdequacyGate | None:
716:    if not isinstance(raw, Mapping):
717:        return None
718:    return AdequacyGate(
719:        decision=str(raw.get("decision", "")),
720:        findings_ok=int(raw.get("findings_ok", 0)),
721:        findings_total=int(raw.get("findings_total", 0)),
722:        critical_count=int(raw.get("critical_count", 0)),
723:    )

726:def _parse_corpus_approval(raw: Any) -> CorpusApprovalGate | None:
727:    """Load corpus_approval.json. Returns None if file is absent."""
728:    if not isinstance(raw, Mapping):
729:        return None
730:    return CorpusApprovalGate(
731:        approved=bool(raw.get("approved", False)),
732:        decision_at_iso=str(raw.get("decision_at_iso", "")),
733:        user_note=str(raw.get("user_note", "")),
734:        approved_count=len(raw.get("approved_source_urls") or []),
735:        rejected_count=len(raw.get("rejected_source_urls") or []),
736:    )

738:
739:def _parse_completeness_percent(raw: Any) -> float:
740:    """Compute completeness as a percentage, 0.0–100.0.
741:
742:    V30 manifests use either:
743:      - {covered_fraction: 0.0–1.0}
744:      - {total_covered: int, total_applicable: int}
745:      - older keys: {covered_topics, total_topics} or {covered, total}
746:
747:    Codex M-1 review caught that the old code looked for keys that don't
748:    exist in the V30 schema, returning 0.0 for a 7/7 manifest.
749:    """
750:    if not isinstance(raw, Mapping):
751:        return 0.0
752:    if "covered_fraction" in raw:
753:        try:
754:            return float(raw["covered_fraction"]) * 100.0
755:        except (TypeError, ValueError):
756:            pass
757:    for cov_key, tot_key in (
758:        ("total_covered", "total_applicable"),
759:        ("covered_topics", "total_topics"),
760:        ("covered", "total"),
761:    ):
762:        if cov_key in raw and tot_key in raw:
763:            try:
764:                cov = float(raw[cov_key])
765:                tot = float(raw[tot_key])
766:                if tot > 0:
767:                    return (cov / tot) * 100.0
768:            except (TypeError, ValueError):
769:                continue
770:    return 0.0
771:
772:
773:def _parse_manifest(raw: Mapping[str, Any]) -> RunManifest:
774:    _require_keys(
775:        raw,
776:        (
777:            "run_id",
778:            "slug",
779:            "status",
780:            "question",
781:            "protocol_sha256",
782:            "evaluator_gate",
783:            "completeness",
784:        ),
785:        "manifest.json",
786:    )
787:    generator = raw.get("generator", {})
788:    if not isinstance(generator, Mapping):
789:        generator = {}
790:    return RunManifest(
791:        run_id=str(raw["run_id"]),
792:        slug=str(raw["slug"]),
793:        status=str(raw["status"]),
794:        question=str(raw["question"]),
795:        protocol_sha256=str(raw["protocol_sha256"]),
796:        cost_usd=float(raw.get("cost_usd", 0.0)),
797:        budget_cap_usd=float(raw.get("budget_cap_usd", 0.0)),
798:        word_count=int(generator.get("words", 0)),
799:        sentences_verified=int(generator.get("sentences_verified", 0)),
800:        sentences_dropped=int(generator.get("sentences_dropped", 0)),
801:        contradictions_found=int(raw.get("contradictions_found", 0)),
802:        completeness_percent=_parse_completeness_percent(raw["completeness"]),
803:        evaluator_gate=_parse_evaluator_gate(raw["evaluator_gate"]),
804:        release_allowed=bool(raw.get("release_allowed", False)),
805:        v30_enabled=bool(raw.get("v30_enabled", False)),
806:        v30_warnings=tuple(str(w) for w in raw.get("v30_warnings", [])),
807:        retrieval_stats=_parse_retrieval_stats(raw.get("retrieval")),
808:    )

811:def _parse_verification_token(raw: Mapping[str, Any]) -> EvidenceSpanToken:
812:    return EvidenceSpanToken(
813:        evidence_id=str(raw.get("evidence_id", "")),
814:        start=int(raw.get("start", 0)),
815:        end=int(raw.get("end", 0)),
816:    )

818:
819:def _parse_verification_sentence(
820:    raw: Mapping[str, Any], section_title: str, status: str, idx: int
821:) -> ReportSentence:
822:    return ReportSentence(
823:        claim_id=f"{section_title}:{status}:{idx}",
824:        section=section_title,
825:        text=str(raw.get("sentence", "")),
826:        tokens=tuple(
827:            _parse_verification_token(t) for t in raw.get("tokens", [])
828:        ),
829:        is_verified=(status == "kept"),
830:        failure_reasons=tuple(str(r) for r in raw.get("failure_reasons", [])),
840:        title = str(sec.get("title", ""))
841:        kept_raw = sec.get("kept", []) or []
842:        dropped_raw = sec.get("dropped", []) or []
843:        sentences = list(
844:            _parse_verification_sentence(s, title, "kept", i)
845:            for i, s in enumerate(kept_raw)
846:        )
847:        sentences.extend(
848:            _parse_verification_sentence(s, title, "dropped", i)
849:            for i, s in enumerate(dropped_raw)
850:        )
851:        sections.append(
852:            ReportSection(
853:                title=title,
854:                kept_count=int(sec.get("total_kept", len(kept_raw))),
855:                dropped_count=int(sec.get("total_dropped", len(dropped_raw))),
856:                total_in=int(sec.get("total_in", 0)),
857:                dropped_due_to_failure=int(sec.get("dropped_due_to_failure", 0)),
858:                sentences=tuple(sentences),
859:            )
860:        )
861:    totals = raw.get("totals", {}) or {}
862:    drop_counts = raw.get("drop_reason_counts", {}) or {}
863:    return VerifiedReport(
864:        sections=tuple(sections),
865:        sentences_verified=int(totals.get("sentences_verified", 0)),
866:        sentences_dropped=int(totals.get("sentences_dropped", 0)),
867:        drop_reason_counts=MappingProxyType(
868:            {str(k): int(v) for k, v in drop_counts.items()}
869:        ),
870:    )

873:def _parse_rule_check(raw: Mapping[str, Any]) -> RuleCheck:
874:    return RuleCheck(
875:        item_id=str(raw.get("item_id", "")),
876:        name=str(raw.get("name", "")),
877:        passed=bool(raw.get("passed", False)),
878:        details=str(raw.get("details", "")),
879:    )

882:def _parse_model_provenance(
883:    eval_rules_raw: Mapping[str, Any] | None,
884:    qwen_judge_raw: Mapping[str, Any] | None,
885:) -> ModelProvenance | None:
886:    """Codex M-1 v2 review fix: load model/version provenance from runtime artifacts.
887:
888:    Returns None if BOTH files are absent (legacy runs predate them).
889:    Raises AuditIRSchemaError if exactly one is present, since partial
890:    provenance silently zero-filling the missing half violates audit-grade
891:    discipline (Codex M-1 v3 review edge case).
892:    """
893:    if eval_rules_raw is None and qwen_judge_raw is None:
894:        return None
895:    if eval_rules_raw is None or qwen_judge_raw is None:
896:        present = "evaluator_rule_checks.json" if eval_rules_raw else "qwen_judge_output.json"
897:        missing = "qwen_judge_output.json" if eval_rules_raw else "evaluator_rule_checks.json"
898:        raise AuditIRSchemaError(
899:            f"Partial model provenance: {present} present but {missing} missing. "
900:            f"Both must be present together or both absent."
901:        )
902:    rule_checks = tuple(
903:        _parse_rule_check(rc) for rc in eval_rules_raw.get("rule_checks", [])
904:    )
905:    return ModelProvenance(
906:        generator_family=str(eval_rules_raw.get("generator_family", "")),
907:        generator_model=str(eval_rules_raw.get("generator_model", "")),
908:        evaluator_family=str(eval_rules_raw.get("evaluator_family", "")),
909:        evaluator_model=str(eval_rules_raw.get("evaluator_model", "")),
910:        judge_model=str(qwen_judge_raw.get("model", "")),
911:        judge_parse_ok=bool(qwen_judge_raw.get("parse_ok", False)),
912:        judge_input_tokens=int(qwen_judge_raw.get("input_tokens", 0)),
913:        judge_output_tokens=int(qwen_judge_raw.get("output_tokens", 0)),
914:        contradictions_disclosed=int(eval_rules_raw.get("contradictions_disclosed", 0)),
915:        contradictions_missing=tuple(
916:            str(c) for c in eval_rules_raw.get("contradictions_missing", [])
917:        ),
918:        rule_checks=rule_checks,
919:    )

928:    )

151:# BUG-B-101 fix: unified manifest.status taxonomy.
152:#
153:# Pre-fix, successful runs wrote manifest.json WITHOUT a "status" key
154:# while abort runs DID include it. Documentation claimed
155:# manifest.status was authoritative — it wasn't. See
156:# outputs/codex_findings/deep_dive_round_1/findings.md for the full
157:# scoping analysis.
158:#
159:# Post-fix, EVERY exit path writes a manifest.json with a "status"
160:# field from this unified taxonomy. `summary["status"]` is preserved
161:# as secondary sweep telemetry for backward compatibility with any
162:# downstream counter that relied on the legacy labels.
163:# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
164:
165:UNIFIED_STATUS_VALUES: frozenset[str] = frozenset({
166:    # success
167:    "success",
168:    # partial — report produced but degraded signal
169:    "partial_thin_corpus",
170:    "partial_incomplete_corpus",
171:    "partial_rule_check_warnings",
172:    "partial_outline_fallback",      # BUG-M-203: planner failed, fallback used
173:    "partial_qwen_advisory",         # BUG-M-205: Qwen judge flagged critical axes
174:    # abort — pipeline refused to produce a report
175:    "abort_scope_rejected",
176:    "abort_no_sources",
177:    "abort_corpus_inadequate",
178:    "abort_corpus_approval_denied",
179:    "abort_no_verified_sections",
180:    "abort_evaluator_critical",      # BUG-M-205: PT08/PT11/PT12 integrity failure
181:    # error — unhandled exception
182:    "error_unexpected",
183:})

204:def to_unified_status(summary_status: str) -> str:
205:    """Map a legacy summary["status"] label to the unified
206:    manifest.status taxonomy. Unknown labels become error_unexpected
207:    (fail loudly for the reader; still a valid taxonomy value)."""
208:    return _SUMMARY_TO_UNIFIED.get(summary_status, "error_unexpected")

211:def expected_str_for_abort(protocol: dict) -> str:
212:    """Render expected tier distribution for abort-artifact text."""
213:    parts = []
214:    for entry in protocol.get("expected_tier_distribution", []) or []:
215:        tier = entry.get("tier")
216:        mn = (entry.get("min_fraction", 0) or 0) * 100
217:        mx = (entry.get("max_fraction", 1) or 1) * 100
218:        if tier:
219:            parts.append(f"{tier} {mn:.0f}-{mx:.0f}%")
220:    return ", ".join(parts) or "per scope template"

222:
223:def _base_manifest_envelope(
224:    *,
225:    run_id: str,
226:    q: dict,
227:    retrieval=None,
228:    run_cost: float = 0.0,
229:) -> dict:
230:    """BUG-SCHEMA-R8d fix: every manifest (success AND abort) carries
231:    the same envelope so downstream consumers get consistent keys
232:    regardless of exit path.
233:
234:    Caller adds status-specific fields on top (adequacy, generator,
235:    evaluator_gate, scope, error, etc.). Using this helper prevents
236:    the envelope from drifting between exit paths.
237:    """
238:    env: dict = {
239:        "run_id": run_id,
240:        "slug": q.get("slug", ""),
241:        "domain": q.get("domain", ""),
242:        "question": q.get("question", ""),
243:        "cost_usd": run_cost,
244:        "budget_cap_usd": PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN,
245:    }
246:    if retrieval is not None:
247:        env["retrieval"] = {
248:            "pre_filter": getattr(retrieval, "total_candidates_pre_filter", 0),
249:            "fetched": getattr(retrieval, "candidates_fetched", 0),
250:            "failed": getattr(retrieval, "candidates_failed_fetch", 0),
251:            "api_calls": getattr(retrieval, "api_calls", {}),
252:        }
253:    return env

src\polaris_graph\generator\multi_section_generator.py:237:- **M-40: Mechanism section is the narrative-depth lever.** When AT LEAST 3 evidence rows in the summary above contain mechanism-of-action vocabulary — in either the `title:` field or the statement body — you MUST include "Mechanism" as one of the outline sections (5 by default, 6 when regulatory evidence is also present per M-41a above). Trigger vocabulary (any of, case-insensitive): "mechanism", "pharmacokinetic", "pharmacodynamic", "receptor", "half-life", "bioavailability", "metabolism", "agonist", "antagonist", "binding", "signaling", "pathway", "kinetic". A research-grade synthesis explains WHY the intervention works, not only WHETHER it works. Top-tier Deep Research outputs (GPT-5.4 DR, Gemini 3.1 Pro DR) dedicate a full section to mechanism/pharmacology for any clinical efficacy question; a report without it reads as a short brief rather than a deep synthesis. This rule is generalizable: in materials/chemistry a Mechanism section covers reaction pathway / phase transition / interface chemistry; in policy it covers causal pathway / incentive mechanism / enforcement mechanism; in finance it covers transmission channel / market microstructure.
src\polaris_graph\generator\multi_section_generator.py:669:12. **Primary-study framing (M-32, for claim-frame rigor)**: When you name a primary study, trial, cohort, experiment, or any individually identifiable empirical data source, and the cited evidence rows contain the structured metadata, provide the study's FULL FRAME in the FIRST sentence that introduces it: (a) sample size or cohort size (e.g. N=1879), (b) baseline value of the outcome being discussed (e.g. mean baseline [PRIMARY_METRIC]=[VALUE], baseline [SECONDARY_METRIC]=[VALUE]), (c) comparator / control / background condition (e.g. versus [COMPARATOR], versus placebo on [BACKGROUND], versus standard [REFERENCE_CONDITION]), and (d) the primary endpoint + timepoint (e.g. [ENDPOINT] change at [TIMEPOINT], [N-YEAR] [OUTCOME_TYPE], cycle-life to [THRESHOLD] retention). If the evidence row carries this structured metadata, you MUST emit it in that first sentence — do not compress N/baseline/endpoint into a single percent-reduction when the evidence carries the full frame. This is what distinguishes a research-grade deep synthesis from a news-style summary. Example template: "In [STUDY NAME], [STUDY_DESIGN_SUMMARY] randomized N=[SAMPLE_SIZE] participants with baseline [OUTCOME]=[BASELINE_VALUE] to [INTERVENTION] versus [COMPARATOR]; [PRIMARY_ENDPOINT] at [TIMEPOINT] was [RESULT] [ev_X]." Subsequent sentences about the same study may reference it by short name without re-framing. Generalizable beyond clinical: a materials paper gets composition + baseline performance + test condition + measured outcome; a cohort study gets population + baseline metric + intervention + outcome; a financial filing gets period + baseline metric + policy/benchmark + reported outcome.
src\polaris_graph\generator\multi_section_generator.py:670:12b. **Claim-frame hard constraint (M-38, eliminating under-framed study mentions)**: Rule #12 is asymmetric and STRICT — when you name a specific study, trial, cohort, or experiment by its short name (phase-N trial identifier in clinical; long-run battery cycling test in materials; named longitudinal cohort in epidemiology; named regulatory docket in policy), that sentence — or the IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING sentence in the same paragraph — MUST carry at LEAST THREE frame elements drawn from: sample size / cohort N; baseline value; comparator / control arm; specific dose or intervention level; primary endpoint; timepoint; effect size WITH uncertainty (CI, SD, or p-value). If you cannot produce three of those elements from the cited evidence, DO NOT name the study by its short name — phrase the sentence generically as "one randomized trial showed ... [ev_X]" or "a prospective cohort in the target population reported ... [ev_X]" or "one pooled analysis found ... [ev_X]" or "a long-run cycling test reported ... [ev_X]" instead. This hard floor prevents the failure mode where a sentence names a specific study but gives only a single effect-size number without N, baseline, or comparator — producing a news-style summary mis-labelled with a primary-study name. Concrete templates (use placeholders): GOOD: "In [STUDY NAME] (N=[SAMPLE_SIZE], baseline [PRIMARY_METRIC]=[BASELINE_VALUE]), [INTERVENTION_ARM] reduced [ENDPOINT] by [EFFECT_SIZE] versus [COMPARATOR_ARM] at [TIMEPOINT] [ev_X]." GOOD (generic when frame is unavailable): "A pre-planned pooled analysis of two phase-3 trials reported [ENDPOINT]=[VALUE] at [TIMEPOINT] [ev_X]." — no short-name attribution because pooled data lack per-trial N. BAD (under-framed, must be rewritten): "[STUDY NAME] showed that [INTERVENTION] reduced [ENDPOINT] more than [COMPARATOR] [ev_X]." — names the study with only one frame element (effect direction); rewrite as "A head-to-head trial of [INTERVENTION] versus [COMPARATOR] reported greater [ENDPOINT] reduction with [INTERVENTION] [ev_X]" which drops the study name because the frame is too thin. BAD (under-framed, must be rewritten): "[STUDY NAME] found median time to [THRESHOLD] was [TIMEPOINT] [ev_X]." — names study with only endpoint + effect; rewrite as "One pooled analysis of two phase-3 trials found median time to [THRESHOLD] was [TIMEPOINT] [ev_X]" ONLY if the cited evidence confirms pooled data across two trials. This rule is what converts a LOSE_BOTH on Claim frames into a competitive synthesis.
src\polaris_graph\generator\multi_section_generator.py:671:12c. **Anaphoric and group claim-frame enforcement (M-42a, extending rule #12b to bypass patterns)**: Rule #12b fires only on explicit short-name study tokens (e.g. specific phase-3 trial identifiers like [STUDY NAME]-N). Sentences using ANAPHORIC references ("This trial", "The same trial", "The study also reported", "That analysis") or GROUP references ("the [PROGRAM] trials", "the phase-3 program", "pivotal trials") bypass that rule and reintroduce the under-framed pattern. This extension closes the bypass:  (A) An ANAPHORIC sentence referring to a specific study must EITHER (a) include at least ONE frame element (sample size, baseline, comparator, dose, endpoint, timepoint, or effect-size-with-uncertainty) in the SAME sentence, OR (b) be placed IMMEDIATELY AFTER a sentence that names the specific study with >=3 frame elements (the antecedent provides framing context). A bare anaphoric sentence with no antecedent framing context is FORBIDDEN. (B) A GROUP reference like "the [PROGRAM] trials" or "the phase-3 program" does NOT inherit from a single prior study's framing. The sentence must EITHER (a) ENUMERATE the specific studies inline — e.g. "the [PROGRAM] trials ([STUDY]-1, -2, -3) pooled N=[SAMPLE_SIZE]" — OR (b) present a pooled / program-level claim with POOLED N AND POOLED effect size stated inline (e.g. "across the [N_TRIALS] pivotal trials pooled N=[SAMPLE_SIZE] adults with [CONDITION], [ENDPOINT] reduction was [EFFECT_SIZE]"). Both parts of the rule apply across domains: in materials/chemistry "these composites" or "the second-gen samples" inherit similarly; in policy "the CMS rules" or "the parallel rulemakings" do. Concrete examples (placeholders only): GOOD: "In [STUDY NAME] (N=[SAMPLE_SIZE], baseline [METRIC]=[BASELINE_VALUE]), [INTERVENTION_ARM] reduced [ENDPOINT] by [EFFECT_SIZE] versus [COMPARATOR_ARM] at [TIMEPOINT] [ev_X]. The same trial also reported [SECONDARY_ENDPOINT]=[VALUE] at [TIMEPOINT] [ev_X]." — second sentence is anaphoric but inherits frame from first. GOOD: "Across the [STUDY]-1, -2, -3, and -4 pooled population (N=[SAMPLE_SIZE]), median time to [THRESHOLD] was [TIMEPOINT] [ev_X]." — group reference with pooled N inline. BAD: "This trial also reported maintained [ENDPOINT] [ev_X]." — anaphoric sentence with no antecedent frame. BAD: "The [PROGRAM] trials found greater [ENDPOINT] reduction with [INTERVENTION] [ev_X]." — group reference without enumeration or pooled N.

 succeeded in 1527ms:
1:"""ScopeDecision — what the scope gate decided about a question.
2:
3:Phase 0/1 stub: deterministic rule-based scoper. Phase 1 swap to the
4:existing scope_eligibility_classifier (LLM-augmented) once the cluster
5:is live and OpenRouter is wired.
6:"""
7:
8:from __future__ import annotations
9:
10:from typing import Literal
11:
12:from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
13:
14:ScopeVerdict = Literal["accepted", "needs_clarification", "rejected"]
15:RefusalReason = Literal[
16:    "clinical_treatment_recommendation",
17:    "individual_legal_advice",
18:    "individual_financial_advice",
19:    "personal_political_endorsement",
20:    "out_of_template_scope",
21:]
22:
23:
24:class ScopeDecision(BaseModel):
25:    verdict: ScopeVerdict
26:    template: str
27:    question: str
28:    rationale: str = Field(..., min_length=8)
29:    refusals: list[RefusalReason] = Field(
30:        default_factory=list,
31:        description=(
32:            "What the gate will refuse if asked downstream. Surfaced in "
33:            "F1 panel so the user knows the boundary before any cost."
34:        ),
35:    )
36:    intended_source_tiers: list[Literal["T1", "T2", "T3"]] = Field(
37:        ...,
38:        description="Which tiers of source the run will draw from.",
39:    )
40:
41:
42:_REFUSAL_PATTERNS: dict[str, RefusalReason] = {
43:    " should i take ": "clinical_treatment_recommendation",
44:    " is this drug right for me": "clinical_treatment_recommendation",
45:    " can i sue ": "individual_legal_advice",
46:    " should i invest ": "individual_financial_advice",
47:    " who should i vote ": "personal_political_endorsement",
48:}
49:
50:
51:def classify_scope(template: str, question: str) -> ScopeDecision:
52:    """Phase 0/1 deterministic scoper — to be replaced by LLM classifier."""
53:    normalized = f" {question.strip().lower()} "
54:
55:    refusals: list[RefusalReason] = []
56:    for pattern, reason in _REFUSAL_PATTERNS.items():
57:        if pattern in normalized:
58:            refusals.append(reason)
59:
60:    if refusals:
61:        return ScopeDecision(
62:            verdict="rejected",
63:            template=template,
64:            question=question,
65:            rationale=(
66:                "Question reads as a personal recommendation request; "
67:                "POLARIS is a research-synthesis system, not a "
68:                "personal-advice service."
69:            ),
70:            refusals=refusals,
71:            intended_source_tiers=[],
72:        )
73:
74:    if len(question.strip()) < 12:
75:        return ScopeDecision(
76:            verdict="needs_clarification",
77:            template=template,
78:            question=question,
79:            rationale=(
80:                "Question is too short to fix scope. Please add the "
81:                "specific timeframe, jurisdiction, or sub-topic you want."
82:            ),
83:            refusals=[],
84:            intended_source_tiers=["T1", "T2"],
85:        )
86:
87:    return ScopeDecision(
88:        verdict="accepted",
89:        template=template,
90:        question=question,
91:        rationale=(
92:            f"Question fits the {template} template. POLARIS will draw "
93:            "from primary-source government and academic data."
94:        ),
95:        refusals=[],
96:        intended_source_tiers=["T1", "T2", "T3"],
97:    )

 succeeded in 1143ms:
1:"""Bundle builder — end-to-end audit bundle assembler.
2:
3:Per `.codex/slices/slice_004/architecture_proposal.md` §"bundle_builder".
4:
5:Pipeline:
6:    ScopeDecision + EvidencePool + VerifiedReport (verdict=success)
7:        ↓
8:    build_manifest_and_files()  -> manifest + content files
9:        ↓
10:    serialize_manifest_yaml()    -> manifest.yaml bytes
11:        ↓
12:    sign_fn(manifest.yaml)       -> manifest.yaml.asc bytes
13:        ↓
14:    pack_tarball()               -> audit_<bundle_id>.tar.gz
15:
16:The output tarball:
17:    audit_<bundle_id>/
18:      manifest.yaml
19:      manifest.yaml.asc
20:      scope_decision.json
21:      evidence_pool.json
22:      verified_report.json
23:      sources/<source_id>.txt   (for each cited source)
24:      metadata.json
25:
26:External verifiers run:
27:    tar -xzf audit_<id>.tar.gz
28:    cd audit_<id>
29:    gpg --verify manifest.yaml.asc manifest.yaml
30:    # then re-hash each content file and compare against manifest.files[*].sha256
31:"""
32:
33:from __future__ import annotations
34:
35:import io
36:import logging
37:import tarfile
38:import time
39:from pathlib import Path
40:from typing import Callable
41:
42:from polaris_graph.audit_bundle.bundle_schema import BundleManifest
43:from polaris_graph.audit_bundle.manifest_builder import (
44:    build_manifest_and_files,
45:    serialize_manifest_yaml,
46:)
47:from polaris_graph.generator2.verified_report import VerifiedReport
48:from polaris_graph.retrieval2.evidence_pool import EvidencePool
49:from polaris_graph.scope.scope_decision import ScopeDecision
50:
51:_LOG = logging.getLogger(__name__)
52:
53:
54:# Type alias for the sign function we accept (Protocol-style duck typing).
55:# Concrete impl is GPGSigner from gpg_signer.py; tests can pass a stub.
56:SignFn = Callable[[bytes], bytes]
57:
58:
59:def _default_sign_fn(_payload: bytes) -> bytes:
60:    """Sentinel that refuses to run.
61:
62:    Forces callers to provide a real signer (or test stub). Per LAW II,
63:    silently writing an unsigned bundle is FORBIDDEN — the audit bundle's
64:    legal value comes from the signature.
65:    """
66:    raise NotImplementedError(
67:        "no sign_fn injected. The audit bundle MUST be GPG-signed. "
68:        "Inject a real signer (e.g. GPGSigner from gpg_signer.py) or "
69:        "a test stub."
70:    )
71:
72:
73:def build_audit_bundle(
74:    decision: ScopeDecision,
75:    pool: EvidencePool,
76:    report: VerifiedReport,
77:    output_dir: Path,
78:    sign_fn: SignFn = _default_sign_fn,
79:) -> Path:
80:    """End-to-end: build manifest + sign + pack tarball.
81:
82:    Args:
83:        decision: slice 001 ScopeDecision
84:        pool: slice 002 EvidencePool (must match report.pool_id)
85:        report: slice 003 VerifiedReport (must have verdict=success)
86:        output_dir: directory where audit_<id>.tar.gz is written
87:        sign_fn: function that takes manifest.yaml bytes and returns
88:                 .asc bytes. Default raises NotImplementedError; callers
89:                 MUST inject a real signer.
90:
91:    Returns:
92:        Path to the generated audit_<bundle_id>.tar.gz file.
93:
94:    Raises:
95:        ValueError: if report.pipeline_verdict != 'success' or pool/report
96:                    FK chain inconsistent.
97:        RuntimeError: if sign_fn raises (propagated, original error chained).
98:    """
99:    if report.pool_id != pool.pool_id:
100:        raise ValueError(
101:            f"FK chain mismatch: report.pool_id={report.pool_id!r} != "
102:            f"pool.pool_id={pool.pool_id!r}"
103:        )
104:    if report.decision_id != decision.decision_id:
105:        raise ValueError(
106:            f"FK chain mismatch: report.decision_id={report.decision_id!r}"
107:            f" != decision.decision_id={decision.decision_id!r}"
108:        )
109:
110:    t_start = time.perf_counter()
111:    manifest, content_files = build_manifest_and_files(decision, pool, report)
112:
113:    manifest_yaml = serialize_manifest_yaml(manifest)
114:
115:    try:
116:        manifest_sig = sign_fn(manifest_yaml)
117:    except NotImplementedError:
118:        raise RuntimeError(
119:            "audit bundle requires a sign_fn; default sentinel forbids "
120:            "shipping unsigned bundles per CLAUDE.md LAW II"
121:        )
122:    except Exception as exc:
123:        raise RuntimeError(
124:            f"sign_fn raised {type(exc).__name__}: {exc}"
125:        ) from exc
126:
127:    # Pack everything into a single .tar.gz under top-level dir audit_<id>
128:    output_dir = Path(output_dir)
129:    output_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
130:    bundle_filename = f"audit_{manifest.bundle_id}.tar.gz"
131:    bundle_path = output_dir / bundle_filename
132:
133:    with tarfile.open(bundle_path, "w:gz") as tar:
134:        top_dir = f"audit_{manifest.bundle_id}"
135:
136:        # 1. manifest.yaml + manifest.yaml.asc (NOT in manifest.files;
137:        #    they are the manifest itself + signature)
138:        _add_file(tar, f"{top_dir}/manifest.yaml", manifest_yaml)
139:        _add_file(tar, f"{top_dir}/manifest.yaml.asc", manifest_sig)
140:
141:        # 2. Content files (paths from manifest.files)
142:        for path, content_bytes in content_files.items():
143:            _add_file(tar, f"{top_dir}/{path}", content_bytes)
144:
145:    elapsed_ms = int((time.perf_counter() - t_start) * 1000)
146:    _LOG.info(
147:        "audit bundle built bundle_id=%s files=%d size=%dB elapsed=%dms",
148:        manifest.bundle_id,
149:        len(manifest.files),
150:        bundle_path.stat().st_size,
151:        elapsed_ms,
152:    )
153:    return bundle_path
154:
155:
156:def _add_file(tar: tarfile.TarFile, name: str, data: bytes) -> None:
157:    """Add bytes to a tarfile under `name`."""
158:    info = tarfile.TarInfo(name=name)
159:    info.size = len(data)
160:    info.mtime = int(time.time())
161:    info.mode = 0o644
162:    tar.addfile(info, io.BytesIO(data))
163:
164:
165:def extract_manifest_from_bundle(
166:    bundle_path: Path,
167:) -> tuple[BundleManifest, bytes, bytes]:
168:    """Helper for verifiers/tests: extract manifest + signature + their bytes.
169:
170:    Returns:
171:        (manifest, manifest_yaml_bytes, manifest_sig_bytes)
172:
173:    Raises FileNotFoundError if either is missing in the tarball.
174:    """
175:    bundle_path = Path(bundle_path)
176:    manifest_yaml: bytes | None = None
177:    manifest_sig: bytes | None = None
178:
179:    with tarfile.open(bundle_path, "r:gz") as tar:
180:        for member in tar.getmembers():

 succeeded in 1462ms:
1:"""Manifest builder — collects all bundle content + computes SHA256s.
2:
3:Per `.codex/slices/slice_004/architecture_proposal.md` §"manifest_builder".
4:
5:Given a ScopeDecision + EvidencePool + VerifiedReport (the research chain
6:output), serializes each to canonical JSON, snapshots cited source
7:texts, and assembles a BundleManifest with SHA256 anchors over every
8:content file.
9:
10:The manifest itself is YAML-serialized (canonical sort keys); GPG signs
11:that YAML. External verifiers re-serialize the manifest and re-verify
12:the signature.
13:"""
14:
15:from __future__ import annotations
16:
17:import hashlib
18:import json
19:from datetime import datetime, timezone
20:from typing import Any
21:
22:import yaml
23:
24:from polaris_graph.audit_bundle.bundle_schema import (
25:    BUNDLE_VERSION,
26:    BundleManifest,
27:    FileEntry,
28:)
29:from pathlib import Path
30:
31:from polaris_graph.audit_bundle.snapshot_sources import (
32:    snapshot_sources,
33:    snapshot_sources_with_reachable,
34:)
35:from polaris_graph.audit_bundle.sovereignty_guard import (
36:    assert_all_pool_sources_legal_cleared,
37:)
38:from polaris_graph.generator2.provenance import extract_tokens
39:from polaris_graph.generator2.verified_report import VerifiedReport
40:from polaris_graph.retrieval2.evidence_pool import EvidencePool
41:from polaris_graph.scope.scope_decision import ScopeDecision
42:
43:
44:POLARIS_VERSION = "6.2.0"
45:
46:# Canonical filenames inside the bundle. External verifiers depend on
47:# these; do not rename without bumping bundle_version.
48:FILE_SCOPE_DECISION = "scope_decision.json"
49:FILE_EVIDENCE_POOL = "evidence_pool.json"
50:FILE_VERIFIED_REPORT = "verified_report.json"
51:FILE_METADATA = "metadata.json"
52:FILE_REVIEWER_README = "REVIEWER_README.md"
53:SOURCES_DIR = "sources"
54:
55:
56:def _sha256_bytes(data: bytes) -> str:
57:    return hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
58:
59:
60:def _serialize_json_canonical(model: Any) -> bytes:
61:    """Pydantic model -> deterministic UTF-8 JSON bytes.
62:
63:    sort_keys + no extra whitespace beyond standard separators ensures
64:    re-serialization on the verifier side produces identical bytes →
65:    SHA256 stable across implementations.
66:    """
67:    if hasattr(model, "model_dump"):
68:        payload = model.model_dump(mode="json")
69:    else:
70:        payload = model
71:    return json.dumps(
72:        payload,
73:        sort_keys=True,
74:        ensure_ascii=False,
75:        separators=(",", ":"),
76:    ).encode("utf-8")
77:
78:
79:def _assert_cited_spans_reachable(report: VerifiedReport, snapshots: dict) -> None:
80:    """Raise ValueError if any cited span exceeds its snapshot's reachable_chars.
81:
82:    Validates the UNION of tokens parsed from `sentence.sentence_text` and
83:    `sentence.provenance_tokens`, deduped by `token.raw`. Missing source_id =>
84:    unreachable. Span end > reachable_chars => unreachable.
85:    """
86:    seen: set[str] = set()
87:    for section in report.sections:
88:        if section.section_status == "dropped":
89:            continue
90:        for sentence in section.verified_sentences:
91:            if not sentence.verifier_pass:
92:                continue
93:            tokens = list(extract_tokens(sentence.sentence_text))
94:            for raw in sentence.provenance_tokens:
95:                tokens.extend(extract_tokens(raw))
96:            for tok in tokens:
97:                if tok.raw in seen:
98:                    continue
99:                seen.add(tok.raw)
100:                entry = snapshots.get(tok.source_id)
101:                if entry is None:
102:                    raise ValueError(
103:                        f"cited span unreachable after snapshot: source "
104:                        f"{tok.source_id!r} not in snapshots (token={tok.raw!r})"
105:                    )
106:                if not (0 <= tok.span_start < tok.span_end <= entry.reachable_chars):
107:                    raise ValueError(
108:                        f"cited span unreachable after snapshot: source "
109:                        f"{tok.source_id!r} token={tok.raw!r} span="
110:                        f"{tok.span_start}-{tok.span_end} reachable={entry.reachable_chars}"
111:                    )
112:
113:
114:def _safe_source_filename(source_id: str) -> str:
115:    """Build a tar-safe filename from a source_id.
116:
117:    source_id is opaque (uuid by default, but could be 'src-1' etc). We
118:    sanitize aggressively: only [a-zA-Z0-9_-]. Anything else becomes _.
119:    Length-capped at 100 chars to avoid pathological inputs.
120:    """
121:    safe = "".join(
122:        c if c.isalnum() or c in "-_" else "_" for c in source_id
123:    )[:100]
124:    return f"{SOURCES_DIR}/{safe}.txt"
125:
126:
127:def build_manifest_and_files(
128:    decision: ScopeDecision,
129:    pool: EvidencePool,
130:    report: VerifiedReport,
131:) -> tuple[BundleManifest, dict[str, bytes]]:
132:    """Build the bundle manifest + content-file dict.
133:
134:    Returns:
135:        (manifest, files) where files is {path: content_bytes}. The
136:        caller (bundle_builder) packs files into a tarball and writes
137:        the manifest.yaml + manifest.yaml.asc alongside.
138:
139:    Raises:
140:        ValueError if report.pipeline_verdict != 'success'.
141:    """
142:    if report.pipeline_verdict != "success":
143:        raise ValueError(
144:            f"cannot build audit bundle for verdict "
145:            f"{report.pipeline_verdict!r}; report must be successful"
146:        )
147:
148:    files_bytes: dict[str, bytes] = {}
149:
150:    # 1. Slice 001 ScopeDecision
151:    sd_bytes = _serialize_json_canonical(decision)
152:    files_bytes[FILE_SCOPE_DECISION] = sd_bytes
153:
154:    # 2. Slice 002 EvidencePool
155:    ep_bytes = _serialize_json_canonical(pool)
156:    files_bytes[FILE_EVIDENCE_POOL] = ep_bytes
157:
158:    # 3. Slice 003 VerifiedReport
159:    vr_bytes = _serialize_json_canonical(report)
160:    files_bytes[FILE_VERIFIED_REPORT] = vr_bytes
161:
162:    # 4. Sovereignty: refuse to ship verbatim spans for non-cleared sources
163:    assert_all_pool_sources_legal_cleared(pool)
164:
165:    # 4b. Per-source snapshots + cited-span reachability guard
166:    snapshot_entries = snapshot_sources_with_reachable(report, pool)
167:    _assert_cited_spans_reachable(report, snapshot_entries)
168:    for source_id, entry in snapshot_entries.items():
169:        files_bytes[_safe_source_filename(source_id)] = entry.text.encode("utf-8")
170:    snapshots = {sid: e.text for sid, e in snapshot_entries.items()}
171:
172:    # 4b. REVIEWER_README ships verbatim in every bundle as metadata content_type.
173:    readme_path = Path(__file__).parent / "REVIEWER_README.md"
174:    files_bytes[FILE_REVIEWER_README] = readme_path.read_bytes()
175:
176:    # 5. Bundle metadata (versions + creation timestamp)
177:    metadata = {
178:        "bundle_version": BUNDLE_VERSION,
179:        "polaris_version": POLARIS_VERSION,
180:        "created_at_utc": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat().replace(
181:            "+00:00", "Z"
182:        ),
183:        "generator_model": report.generator_model,
184:        "decision_id": decision.decision_id,
185:        "pool_id": report.pool_id,
186:        "report_id": report.report_id,
187:        "source_snapshot_count": len(snapshots),
188:    }
189:    files_bytes[FILE_METADATA] = json.dumps(
190:        metadata,
191:        sort_keys=True,
192:        ensure_ascii=False,
193:        separators=(",", ":"),
194:    ).encode("utf-8")
195:
196:    # Build FileEntry list with content_type tags
197:    file_entries: list[FileEntry] = []
198:    content_type_by_path = {
199:        FILE_SCOPE_DECISION: "scope_decision",
200:        FILE_EVIDENCE_POOL: "evidence_pool",
201:        FILE_VERIFIED_REPORT: "verified_report",
202:        FILE_METADATA: "metadata",
203:    }
204:    for path, content in files_bytes.items():
205:        ct = content_type_by_path.get(
206:            path,
207:            "source_snapshot" if path.startswith(SOURCES_DIR + "/") else "metadata",
208:        )
209:        file_entries.append(
210:            FileEntry(
211:                path=path,
212:                sha256=_sha256_bytes(content),
213:                size_bytes=len(content),
214:                content_type=ct,  # type: ignore[arg-type]
215:            )
216:        )
217:
218:    # Sort entries deterministically for stable manifest output
219:    file_entries.sort(key=lambda f: f.path)
220:
221:    manifest = BundleManifest(
222:        decision_id=decision.decision_id,
223:        pool_id=report.pool_id,
224:        report_id=report.report_id,
225:        generator_model=report.generator_model,
226:        polaris_version=POLARIS_VERSION,
227:        files=file_entries,
228:    )
229:    return manifest, files_bytes
230:
231:
232:def serialize_manifest_yaml(manifest: BundleManifest) -> bytes:
233:    """Produce canonical YAML bytes for GPG signing.
234:
235:    PyYAML default_flow_style=False + sort_keys=True keeps it stable
236:    across re-serializations.
237:    """
238:    payload = manifest.model_dump(mode="json")
239:    return yaml.safe_dump(
240:        payload,
241:        default_flow_style=False,
242:        sort_keys=True,
243:        allow_unicode=True,
244:    ).encode("utf-8")

1:"""FastAPI route for slice 004 audit-bundle export.
2:
3:POST /api/audit-bundle — accepts a {decision, pool, report} triple
4:(typically the slice 001 + 002 + 003 chain output) and streams back
5:a GPG-signed audit_<bundle_id>.tar.gz file.
6:

41:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

43:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
44:
45:def get_sign_fn() -> SignFn | None:
46:    """Returns the active GPG signer or None.
47:
48:    None signals the orchestrator's sentinel default which raises and is
49:    caught by the route -> HTTP 503. Tests override this dep; production
50:    binds to a real GPGSigner via app.dependency_overrides at startup
51:    when POLARIS_GPG_KEY_ID is set.
52:    """
53:    return None
54:
55:
56:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
57:# Request / response models
58:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
59:
60:class AuditBundleRequest(BaseModel):
61:    """POST body for /api/audit-bundle.
62:
63:    Carries the full research chain (decision + pool + report). The route
64:    re-validates the FK chain consistency before building.
65:    """
66:
67:    decision: ScopeDecision = Field(description="slice 001 ScopeDecision")
68:    pool: EvidencePool = Field(description="slice 002 EvidencePool")
69:    report: VerifiedReport = Field(
70:        description="slice 003 VerifiedReport (verdict=success required)"
71:    )
72:
73:
74:class AuditBundleErrorResponse(BaseModel):
75:    """Body returned with HTTP 4xx/5xx when bundle cannot be assembled."""
76:
77:    error: bool = True
78:    code: str  # 'fk_chain_mismatch' | 'verdict_not_success' |
79:               # 'cited_span_unreachable_after_snapshot' |
80:               # 'copyrighted_span_in_bundle' |
81:               # 'gpg_unavailable' | 'sign_failed'
82:    message: str
83:    report_id: str | None = None
84:
85:
86:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
87:# Routes
88:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
89:
90:@router.post("/audit-bundle")
91:def post_audit_bundle(
92:    req: AuditBundleRequest,
93:    sign_fn: SignFn | None = Depends(get_sign_fn),
94:) -> Any:
95:    """Build + return a GPG-signed audit bundle as application/gzip.
96:
97:    Returns the .tar.gz file as a streaming download.
98:    """
99:    if sign_fn is None:
100:        raise HTTPException(
101:            status_code=503,
102:            detail={
103:                "error": True,
104:                "code": "gpg_unavailable",
105:                "message": (
106:                    "GPG signer not configured. Set POLARIS_GPG_KEY_ID in env "
107:                    "and ensure the corresponding key is in the gpg keyring."
108:                ),
109:                "report_id": req.report.report_id,
110:            },
111:        )
112:
113:    # Build into a temp dir; FileResponse copies it to the wire.
114:    tmp_dir = Path(tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix="polaris_audit_"))
115:    try:
116:        bundle_path = build_audit_bundle(
117:            req.decision,
118:            req.pool,
119:            req.report,
120:            output_dir=tmp_dir,
121:            sign_fn=sign_fn,
122:        )
123:    except ValueError as exc:
124:        # FK chain mismatch, verdict != success, cited-span unreachable, or copyrighted span
125:        msg = str(exc)
126:        if "copyrighted span" in msg:
127:            code = "copyrighted_span_in_bundle"
128:        elif "cited span unreachable" in msg:
129:            code = "cited_span_unreachable_after_snapshot"
130:        elif "FK chain" in msg or "pool_id" in msg or "decision_id" in msg:
131:            code = "fk_chain_mismatch"
132:        else:
133:            code = "verdict_not_success"
134:        raise HTTPException(
135:            status_code=400,
136:            detail={
137:                "error": True,
138:                "code": code,
139:                "message": msg,
140:                "report_id": req.report.report_id,
141:            },
142:        )
143:    except RuntimeError as exc:
144:        raise HTTPException(
145:            status_code=502,
146:            detail={
147:                "error": True,
148:                "code": "sign_failed",
149:                "message": str(exc),
150:                "report_id": req.report.report_id,
151:            },
152:        )
153:
154:    return FileResponse(
155:        path=str(bundle_path),
156:        filename=bundle_path.name,
157:        media_type="application/gzip",
158:    )
159:

208:                "message": msg,

210:            },
211:        )

213:    for entry in manifest.files:
214:        slot = breakdown[entry.content_type]
215:        slot["count"] += 1
216:        slot["bytes"] += entry.size_bytes

219:        "generator_model": manifest.generator_model,
220:        "polaris_version": manifest.polaris_version,
221:        "file_count": len(manifest.files),
222:        "total_bytes": manifest.total_bytes(),
223:        "content_type_breakdown": breakdown,
224:    }

 succeeded in 1608ms:
1:"""Source snapshot — collects every cited source's text into the bundle.
2:
3:Per `.codex/slices/slice_004/architecture_proposal.md` §"snapshot_sources".
4:
5:Walks every kept VerifiedSentence across every non-dropped Section in
6:the VerifiedReport, extracts the unique source_ids referenced by their
7:provenance tokens, then pulls the full_text (or snippet fallback) from
8:the EvidencePool.
9:
10:The snapshotted text is what makes the bundle independently verifiable
11:offline: a third party can re-run strict_verify against the bundle's
12:content without re-fetching from the original source URLs (which may
13:have changed since retrieval).
14:"""
15:
16:from __future__ import annotations
17:
18:from typing import NamedTuple
19:
20:from polaris_graph.generator2.provenance import extract_tokens
21:from polaris_graph.generator2.verified_report import VerifiedReport
22:from polaris_graph.retrieval2.evidence_pool import EvidencePool, Source
23:
24:
25:class SnapshotEntry(NamedTuple):
26:    """Snapshot text plus the reachable character count (excludes appended truncation note)."""
27:
28:    text: str
29:    reachable_chars: int
30:
31:
32:# Cap each source's text to keep the bundle bounded. 200KB per source
33:# is enough for a typical full-text article; a Cochrane systematic
34:# review with all appendices may need more — adjust if production
35:# data shows truncation hits the audit_quality_gate.
36:MAX_SOURCE_TEXT_BYTES = 200 * 1024  # 200 KB
37:
38:SOURCE_TRUNCATION_NOTE_TEMPLATE = (
39:    "\n\n[POLARIS audit-bundle truncation notice: source text truncated "
40:    "from {original_size} to {truncated_size} bytes per "
41:    "MAX_SOURCE_TEXT_BYTES policy.]"
42:)
43:
44:
45:def _cited_source_ids(report: VerifiedReport) -> set[str]:
46:    """Collect source_ids from provenance tokens of every kept sentence
47:    in every non-dropped section.
48:
49:    Dropped sentences are excluded — we don't want to ship snapshots
50:    for sources that didn't survive verification.
51:    """
52:    out: set[str] = set()
53:    for section in report.sections:
54:        if section.section_status == "dropped":
55:            continue
56:        for sentence in section.verified_sentences:
57:            if not sentence.verifier_pass:
58:                continue
59:            for token_str in sentence.provenance_tokens:
60:                tokens = extract_tokens(token_str)
61:                for tok in tokens:
62:                    out.add(tok.source_id)
63:    return out
64:
65:
66:def _snapshot_text(source: Source) -> str:
67:    """Return the text to snapshot for one source.
68:
69:    Prefers full_text; falls back to snippet. Truncates at
70:    MAX_SOURCE_TEXT_BYTES with an audit-visible note appended so
71:    re-verifiers see why the span may not resolve.
72:    """
73:    text = source.full_text if source.full_text is not None else source.snippet
74:    text_bytes = text.encode("utf-8")
75:    if len(text_bytes) <= MAX_SOURCE_TEXT_BYTES:
76:        return text
77:
78:    # Truncate to MAX_SOURCE_TEXT_BYTES respecting UTF-8 boundaries.
79:    truncated = text_bytes[:MAX_SOURCE_TEXT_BYTES]
80:    # Walk back continuation bytes (10xxxxxx). The first non-continuation
81:    # byte we hit is either a single-byte ASCII char (0xxxxxxx, complete)
82:    # or a multi-byte start byte (11xxxxxx). If it's a start byte, the
83:    # codepoint is incomplete because we already trimmed the continuations
84:    # — drop the start byte too.
85:    while truncated and (truncated[-1] & 0xC0) == 0x80:
86:        truncated = truncated[:-1]
87:    if truncated and (truncated[-1] & 0x80) and (truncated[-1] & 0x40):
88:        truncated = truncated[:-1]
89:    note = SOURCE_TRUNCATION_NOTE_TEMPLATE.format(
90:        original_size=len(text_bytes),
91:        truncated_size=len(truncated),
92:    )
93:    return truncated.decode("utf-8") + note
94:
95:
96:def snapshot_sources(
97:    report: VerifiedReport,
98:    pool: EvidencePool,
99:) -> dict[str, str]:
100:    """Return {source_id: snapshot_text} for every source cited in the
101:    report's kept sentences.
102:
103:    Source_ids that appear in tokens but are not present in the pool
104:    are silently skipped (strict_verify already rejected those sentences,
105:    so they shouldn't have made it through; defensive coding).
106:    """
107:    needed = _cited_source_ids(report)
108:    pool_index: dict[str, Source] = {s.source_id: s for s in pool.sources}
109:
110:    out: dict[str, str] = {}
111:    for source_id in needed:
112:        source = pool_index.get(source_id)
113:        if source is None:
114:            # Should not happen post-strict-verify; defensive skip
115:            continue
116:        out[source_id] = _snapshot_text(source)
117:    return out
118:
119:
120:def snapshot_size_bytes(snapshots: dict[str, str]) -> int:
121:    """Total bytes across all snapshots — useful for bundle-size checks."""
122:    return sum(len(text.encode("utf-8")) for text in snapshots.values())
123:
124:
125:def _snapshot_entry(source: Source) -> SnapshotEntry:
126:    """Snapshot one source returning text + reachable_chars (excl. truncation note)."""
127:    text = source.full_text if source.full_text is not None else source.snippet
128:    text_bytes = text.encode("utf-8")
129:    if len(text_bytes) <= MAX_SOURCE_TEXT_BYTES:
130:        return SnapshotEntry(text=text, reachable_chars=len(text))
131:    truncated = text_bytes[:MAX_SOURCE_TEXT_BYTES]
132:    while truncated and (truncated[-1] & 0xC0) == 0x80:
133:        truncated = truncated[:-1]
134:    if truncated and (truncated[-1] & 0x80) and (truncated[-1] & 0x40):
135:        truncated = truncated[:-1]
136:    body = truncated.decode("utf-8")
137:    note = SOURCE_TRUNCATION_NOTE_TEMPLATE.format(
138:        original_size=len(text_bytes), truncated_size=len(truncated)
139:    )
140:    return SnapshotEntry(text=body + note, reachable_chars=len(body))
141:
142:
143:def snapshot_sources_with_reachable(
144:    report: VerifiedReport, pool: EvidencePool
145:) -> dict[str, SnapshotEntry]:
146:    """Like snapshot_sources but returns SnapshotEntry tuples (text + reachable_chars)."""
147:    needed = _cited_source_ids(report)
148:    pool_index: dict[str, Source] = {s.source_id: s for s in pool.sources}
149:    out: dict[str, SnapshotEntry] = {}
150:    for source_id in needed:
151:        source = pool_index.get(source_id)
152:        if source is None:
153:            continue
154:        out[source_id] = _snapshot_entry(source)
155:    return out

codex
The bundle path adds one more hard constraint: bundle assembly validates cited source offsets against the text actually stored in the `EvidencePool`. If the bridge only carries bibliography statements as snippets, original verifier spans can point past the snippet and fail bundle creation. I’m checking a real artifact shape to see whether those spans are bibliography-sized or source-text-sized.

Pharmaceuticals (Basel).2025. ## Abstract **Background:** Obesity and type 2 diabetes (T2D) are major public health concerns. Tirzepatide has shown promise in recent clinical trials. This systematic review and meta-analysis aim to evaluate the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide in adults with obesity or type 2 diabetes, compared to placebo, GLP-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs), and insulin. **Method**: PubMed, Embase, and the Cochrane Library were searched on 17 January 2024, focusing on phase II and III randomized controlled trials (RCTs). Epub 2025 Sep 9. ### Authors [Haixia Guan](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Guan+H&cauthor_id=40926359)[1](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40926359/#short-view-affiliation-1 \"Department of Endocrinology, Guangdong Provincial People's\n\n[...]\n\nli Lilly and Company, Shanghai, China. * 5 Department of Endocrinology, Peking University People's Hospital, Beijing, People's Republic of China. * PMID: **40926359** * PMCID: [PMC12515763](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12515763/) * DOI: [10.1111/dom.70047](https://doi.org/10.1111/dom.70047) Item in Clipboard Full text links Cite Display options Display options Format ## Abstract **Aim:** To evaluate the long-term efficacy and safety data at 104 weeks in tirzepatide-treated participants with type 2 diabetes who had inadequate\n\n[...]\n\no receive tirzepatide (5, 10, or 15 mg) or insulin glargine. The primary efficacy endpoint was change in HbA1c levels from baseline to 104 weeks. Key secondary endpoints were changes in body weight and the proportion of participants achieving HbA1c <7.0%. Safety endpoints included the incidence of treatment-emergent adverse events (AEs) and hypoglycaemia. **Results:** This post-hoc analysis included 1,500 participants. At Week 104, participants in the tirzepatide groups had significantly greater mean reduction in HbA1c (5 mg: -2.3%, 10 mg: -2.5%, 15 mg: -2.6%) compared with the insulin glargine group (-1.0%) (p < 0.001). Participants in the tirzepatide groups had significantly greater reduction in body weight (5 mg: -7.6 kg, 10 mg: -10.0 kg, 15 mg: -11.4 kg) compared with the insulin glargine group (2.1 kg) (p < 0.001). Significantly more participants in the tirzepatide group achieved HbA1c <7.0% compared with the insulin glargine group (p < 0.001). The incidence of hypoglycaemia was lower in the tirzepatide groups, and gastrointestinal AEs were mild or moderate in severity. **Conclusions:** Tirzepatide significantly improved glycaemic control and body weight reduction compared to insulin gla\n\n[...]\n\n study population for change in HbA1c.
